label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer to this question. The question is asking why a novelist can expect the publisher to print the manuscript exactly as written. The options given are A, B, C, and D. First, I'll need to look back at the passage provided to find relevant information. The passage starts by talking about how a novelist's medium is the written word, and that the novel is consumed by a silent, individual reader. It mentions that the paperback novel is the cheapest and most portable form of narrative entertainment. It also states that the novel is limited to a single channel of information—writing. Then it says that the writer is restricted by nothing except purely artistic criteria and that the novelist keeps absolute control over his text until it is published and received by the audience. It also mentions that even a well-established novelist might deliver a manuscript and expect the publisher to print it exactly as written. The passage then contrasts this with playwrights and screenplay writers, who can't expect their scripts to be performed without rewriting because plays and motion pictures are cooperative forms using multiple channels of communication. The key points here are about the control the novelist has over their text. The passage says the novelist keeps absolute control until publication. The question is specifically about why they can expect the manuscript to be printed as written. Looking at the answer choices: A) Because the novelist keeps absolute control over his text. This seems to directly relate to what the passage states. The passage says the novelist has absolute control until it's published. So if they have that control, they can expect the publisher to print it as is. B) Because the paperback novel is most portable and adaptable. The passage mentions this as a characteristic of the paperback, but how does that relate to the publisher printing it as written? It might be a distractor. The portability and adaptability are about the physical form and convenience, not about the control over the text. C) Because the novel is limited to a single channel of information—writing. This is mentioned in the passage, and it's contrasted with plays and films which use multiple channels. The single channel (writing) might mean that there's no need for collaboration, so the novelist can have the text printed as is. This could be a possible answer. The passage later explains that plays involve more than just words (actors, sets, etc.), so they require collaboration and rewriting. But novels, being a single channel, don't need that. So maybe C is also a contender. D) Because the novelist is seldom advised by editors to revise the text. The passage says the writer may be advised by the editor to revise but if they refuse, no one would be surprised. So actually, the novelist might be advised, but they can refuse. The answer D says "seldom advised," but the passage doesn't say that they are seldom advised; it says they might be advised but can refuse. So D is not accurate. Now between A and C. The passage states that the novelist keeps absolute control over the text until it is published. The question is why they can expect the publisher to print it as written. The answer A is because they have absolute control. C is about the single channel of information. The passage does state that the reason playwrights and screenwriters can't expect their work to be performed as written is because they are cooperative forms using more than one channel. So the reason for the novelist's ability to have it printed as is is because it's a single channel (writing) and they have that control. But the passage's direct statement is that the novelist keeps absolute control. The question is why can they expect the publisher to print it as written. The answer is because they have control. The single channel is the reason why they don't need to collaborate (as per the contrast with plays and films). So the answer could be A or C. The passage says, "The novelist keeps absolute control over his text until it is published and received by the audience. He may be advised by his editor to revise his text, but if the writer refused to meet this condition no one would be surprised. It is not unknown for a well-established novelist to deliver his or her manuscript and expect the publisher to print it exactly as written." So the reason why the novelist can expect the publisher to print it as written is because of the absolute control they have. The fact that it's a single channel (writing) is part of the explanation for why that control exists, but the direct answer to the question is A.
